questions.description: Answer Part (A) AND Part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key functions in the creation and application of European…
questions.text: Answer Part (A) AND Part (B) (A) Outline and discuss the key functions in the creation and application of European Union law of (i) The European Commission, (ii) The European Parliament and (iii) the Council AND (B) Assess the key differences between the legal nature of those laws that are introduced into national law by a directive and those which are introduced by a regulation, as set out in Article 288 TFEU.

questions.description: How are the 'general principles' of EU law such as fundamental rights, legal certainty, non-discrimination and…
questions.text: How are the 'general principles' of EU law such as fundamental rights, legal certainty, non-discrimination and proportionality recognised and used by the Court of Justice in its interpretation and application of EU law?

questions.description: In January 2016, a (fictional) regulation, Regulation (EU) No 26/2016 of… Regulation.
questions.text: In January 2016, a (fictional) regulation, Regulation (EU) No 26/2016 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 January 2016 on setting maximum permitted power usage levels for household appliances, was published in the Official Journal. Article 55 of Regulation 26/2016 provides that: 'This Regulation shall enter into force on the 20th day following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union [and] it shall apply from 12 March 2016, with the exception of Article 10(2) which shall apply from 13 August 2016'. Article 10(2) of the same regulation states that: 'Household vacuum cleaners shall have a maximum permitted input power of 1300 watts'. Virginia Vacuums, based in Co Cavan have manufactured budget-priced household vacuum cleaners since 1997. All of their products have an input power level of 1400 watts or greater and are significantly less expensive that most of their competitor's products. As a result they have become very competitive with consumers in those EU member states where they are sold/ Their main markets are in Ireland, the UK, France, Germany, Italy and Poland although they do sell their products in most other Member States as well. They currently have a 40% market share in all vacuum cleaners sold across the EU. 85% of all vacuum cleaners with a power rating above 1400 watts that are sold in the EU Aare manufactured by Virginia Vacuums. This latter market share has been steadily rising since 2008. Virginia Vacuums now seek your advice on whether they have any legal standing to challenge this new Regulation 26/2016 before the General Court of the European Union. Advise Virginia Vacuums on the basis of existing case law and TFEU provisions.

Answer (A) OR (B). Do not answer both parts. (A) Discuss, with reference to relevant case law in particular, the extent to which and the conditions by which EU directives have 'direct effect' in national law. OR (B) Critically assess the jurisprudence of the Court of Justice on the direct effect of International Agreements such as the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) and the World Trade Organisation (WTO) Agreements in European Union and National Law.

questions.description: Pollaphuca Publishing Ltd have been producing and marketing books in… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Pollaphuca Publishing Ltd have been producing and marketing books in Ireland since 1949. Their focus as a company has always been the production of high quality and well-crafted books. All of its products are hard bound with the covers made from leather. Most of the leather that they use for this purpose is imported into Ireland from other EU Member States including Italy, Spain and France. Ireland introduced a new (fictional) law in 2015, the Animal Products (Control of the Spread of Disease) Regulations. The regulations include a provision that requires that all imported leather must be inspected by officials from the Irish Department of Agriculture for possible traces of animal disease before it can be brought into and used in the State. If no traces of any disease are found on inspected leather then the Department of Agriculture will provide certification verifying this. Directive 2011/613 (fictional) of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 April 2011 on measures that they may be adopted to control the spread of disease in bovine animals includes a provision in Article 7 thereof which states that: 'Member States may require the production of a valid certification upon importation from any other Member State or Third Country that any product made from bovine animals is free from any transferrable diseases'. Pollaphuca Publishing imported a consignment of 12 tonnes of leather from Spain in December 2015 which was subjected to the required inspection by the Department of Agriculture. Pollaphuca Publishing have now received a bill from the Department of Agriculture for €2,400 before they will provide the required certification, which they state is to cover the costs of carrying out the inspections and related administrative charges. On the basis of existing case law and TFEU provisions, advise Pollaphuca Publishing Ltd as to whether the carrying out of inspections under the Animal Products (Control of the Spread of Disease) Regulations 2015 and the imposition of related charges is compatible with EU Law.

questions.text: Write a case note on any three of the following eight judgements setting out in each case note the core facts,

questions.description: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU…
questions.text: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the application of EU competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU and the scope and limits to any such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings wo which Member States have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by Member States with the operation of services of general economic interest.

questions.description: Ellen, who will be 47 years old next July has been employed by Cutlery… Company.
questions.text: Ellen, who will be 47 years old next July has been employed by Cutlery Catering Ltd since 2004. She was initially employed as a catering assistant on a part time basis for four years during which time she had no set hours of work but instead would be called in by the company to assist at large functions. This usually involved working one 12 hour shift per week at these functions as well as some additional hours, normally 6 or 7, helping out with the company's book-keeping. Some weeks she had no work at all but she did normally work at least 18-20 hours per week for 32 to 38 weeks of the year. From July 2008 on, Ellen was employed on a full time basis by Cutlery Catering Ltd usually working for at least 49 hours per week. Her salary rose from the part time rate of €11.75 per hour to the full time rate of €14.50 per hour. In April 2010, Cutlery Catering Ltd signed all of their full time employees up to a private pension scheme. This was to be backdated to their starting date of full time employment with the company. Cutlery Catering Ltd made payments to the private pension company to make up for the shortfall in contributions which were not paid for full-time or any employees prior to April 2010. Ellen is concerned that when she retires, in around 20 years time, she will only have made 28 years of contributions to the pension scheme. As this is a defined contribution scheme, where pensions received are based upon years of contributions, she can only receive a maximum pension based on these 28 years (July 2008 until date of compulsory retirement from the company, at 67 years of age -- July 2036) and not the usual maximum of 40 years. Ellen is of the opinion that she should be entitled to a pensionable period that also includes the years 2004-2008 when she was employed by Cutlery Catering on a part-time basis. This would at least raise her pension to one based on 32 years contributions. 70% of all full time employees with Cutlery Catering are male. She has also raised some concerns about the fact that she was paid at a lower rate (hourly) during this period than her full time colleagues (by €2.75 per hour). Her employers have dismissed these concerns stating that they are entitled to offer better conditions of employment to different categories of workers dependent upon their full time or part time status. Advise Ellen as to any possible breaches by Cutlery Catering Ltd of EU law equality.

questions.description: Outline and assess the key changes and developments that have been made by Treaty amendments since the Single European…
questions.text: Outline and assess the key changes and developments that have been made by Treaty amendments since the Single European Act of 1987 to the role of the European Parliament in the EU law making process. In particular, you should make an assessment about whether these changes may be sufficient to address concerns about the 'democratic deficit' which it has been claimed exists in the European Union.

Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. Part (A) The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgement that.... '....in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union Law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order'. How have these principles of equivalence and practical possibility referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law? Assess by reference to relevant case-law. OR Part (B) Article 5 of (the fictional) Directive 2012/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise-levels at outdoor music concerts provides that: ' all licence holders operating outdoor music concerts must ensure that those working at these events are subjected to a maximum noise level of 116 decibels for a maximum continuous period of four hours'. Various methods are set out in the directive for measuring the noise level at these events. A series of definitions is also set out which clearly classify security staff as being amongst those who are 'working at these events' and who are therefore covered by the protection set out in Article 5 of the Directive. Article 12 of the same Directive further provides that: 'member states shall bring into force laws. Regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 31 March 2014'. Prior to the introduction of Directive 2012/456/EU , maximum permitted noise levels for concerts in Ireland were set out in the (fictional) Environmental Noise Act 1994. This 1994 Act allowed for exposure to a maximum noise levels of 125 decibels for a continuous period of up to 6 hours. Brian was employed on a part time basis as a security Guard by Cool Concerts Ltd between January 2013 and October 2015. He worked at outdoor music concert events for an average of four days per month during this period. He was regularly exposed to noise levels which exceeded 116 decibels. At one heavy metal festival at which Brian was working, held in Limerick on 3rd August 2015, the noise levels reached 124 decibels. Brian was exposed to this high noise volume for up to five hours on that day. He has been suffering from tinnitus since this date and he is no longer able to work for Cool Concerts Ltd as a security guard due to the almost constant ringing noise in his ears. Directive 2012/456/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Environmental Noise (Maximum permitted levels at outdoor events) Regulations in May 2016. Brian now seeks your advice on whether he can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Cool Concerts Ltd or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2012 Directive. Your advice should focus on whether or not Brian was protected by the provisions of Directive 2012/456/EU on 3rd August 2015 and if so, whether any remedies may be available to him under EU Law.

questions.description: Article 3 of (fictional) Regulation (EU) No 999/2016 on the use of traffic… Regulation, Company.
questions.text: Article 3 of (fictional) Regulation (EU) No 999/2016 on the use of traffic cones on national primary roads in the internal market sets out the specifications for these products where they are used by national road authorities in all EU member states. It states, amongst other things, that all traffic cones when used on national primary roads must be (i) 95 cm in height (ii) have a base that is 45cm wide (iii) be bright orange in colour and (iv) be covered by a reflecting white band between the 28cm and 42cm vertical points. Saggart Safety Company Ltd have been manufacturing traffic cones since 1977. They are the sole supplier of traffic cones to those responsible for road safety in Ireland, including An Garda Siochana and the Road Safety Authority. Saggart Safety produced 95% of all traffic cones sold in Ireland in 2015. The standards set for traffic cones by Regulation 999/2016 are based on those which are already in use in every EU member state, except for two, Ireland and Malta. Advise Saggart Safety Co Ltd as to whether there is any basis upon which they may possibly have a standing (locus standi) under Article 263 TFEU to challenge the legality of Regulation 999/2016 before the Court of Justice.

questions.description: Referring to the relevant case law and legislation, make an assessment of whether EU law and its implementation by the…
questions.text: Referring to the relevant case law and legislation, make an assessment of whether EU law and its implementation by the Member States is now sufficiently well developed in relation to the issue of sex equality in employment that the focus of the relevant EU institutions can now be shifted to dealing with other forms of discrimination such as that which is based on religious belief, age, race, disability and /or sexual orientation.

questions.description: The Department of Health introduced the (fictional) Water Coolers at Work… Directive.
questions.text: The Department of Health introduced the (fictional) Water Coolers at Work Bill in May 2016 as a direct response to a series of scientific studies which have shown that there has been at least 14 cases of infection, per year for each of the six years, with Hepatitis A in Ireland following the consumption of contaminated water from coolers. Under the provisions set out in the bill, all water used in coolers that is for consumption in places of employment must now be certified as being free from Hepatitis A. Inspections of the containers of water used in coolers are to be carried out by officials from the Irish Department of Health. The inspection and certification process applies to both Irish water and that imported from any other country including all EU member states. The Department of Health is also promoted to impose a charge on producers to cover the costs of these inspections. There is no provision for EU law which provides that water used in coolers must be inspected for safety purposes but Directive 1997/313 (fictional) does allow member states to carry out inspections of any beverages, including water, where they are in possession of evidence showing that there is a potential risk of infection. The EU commission and several EU member states are concerned about the ramifications that the introduction of the Water Coolers at Work Bill 2016 could have for the free movement of bottled water. Advise the Department of Health as to whether the provisions of the 2016 Bill on the inspection, certification and charging of producers for these inspections would, if enacted, be compatible, or otherwise, with the Treaty provisions and related case-law on the free movement of goods -- in particular Articles 30 and 34 TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgements of the Union judicature, setting out in each case note…
questions.text: Write a case note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgements of the Union judicature, setting out in each case note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice in the three judgements chosen: - CASE c-352/98 Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm SA and Goupil v Commission [2000] ECR-I 5291; - Cases C-402 and 415/2005 Yassin Abdullah Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v Council and Commission [2008] ECR-I- 6351; - Case C370/2012 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756; - Case 106/77 Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA [1978] ECR 629; Case C-453/99 Courage Ltd v Crehan [2001] ECR-I 6297; - Case C438/2005 International Transport Workers Federation and Finnish Seamens Union v Viking Line ABP and Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR I- 779; - Case C-236/2009 Association Belge des Consommateurs Test Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I- 773; - Case C-384/93 Alpine Investments v Minister van Finacien [1995] ECR I- 1141.

questions.description: 'The status of EU citizenship created by EU law has been criticised on various grounds, including the thinness of the…
questions.text: 'The status of EU citizenship created by EU law has been criticised on various grounds, including the thinness of the rights created and their economic focus the conditions to which they are subject, the reinforcement of the distinction between third country nationals and EU nationals, the limited impact of the new electoral rights, and the reluctant pace of implementation' Craig and De Burca, EU Law, 6th edition (OUP 2015) Present a discussion on whether, or to what extent, you agree with this statement on the legal nature of EU citizenship, with particular consideration for developments in the case-law of the Court of Justice, such as those delivered in the judgements in Ruiz Zambrano, Metock and Dano.

Answer Part (A) OR Part (B). Do NOT attempt both parts. Part (A) Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, the concepts of 'agreement', 'decision' and 'concerted practice' in Article 101 (1) TFEU. Also, explain, by reference to relevant case law, how a 'dominant position' can be held by 'one or more undertakings' under Article 102 TFEU. OR Part (B) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU. Analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strength or extend the undertaking's dominant market position?
